coredata.dc:title: A facile rapid-response and on-site sensor for tetracycline detection in environmental water based on europium-doped carbonized polymer dots
coredata.dc:creator: Nie, Qi
Deng, Jingjing
Zhou, Tianshu
coredata.dc:description: With the increasing concerns about public safety and environmental protection, it is of great significance to establish a rapid and sensitive method to detect antibiotic residues in environmental samples. Herein, fluorescent carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) were synthesized and used for quantitative assay of tetracycline (TC) by chelating with europium ions (Eu3+). The green fluorescence of CPDs could be gradually quenched by TC via inner filter effect and static quenching mechanism, while the red emission of Eu3+ was remarkably enhanced through energy transfer and antenna effect. This ratiometric fluorescence Eu/CPDs-based sensor displayed a wide detection range of 0.05–56, 56–100 μM with a detection limit of 16.4 nM, and was triumphantly applied for TC analysis in water samples with high reliability and accuracy. Moreover, the polymeric structure in CPDs endowed the sensor with good long-term sensing stability up to 600 days. By integrating Eu/CPDs with portable capillary testing kit, a point-of-use device was constructed for facile determination of TC in river water samples with a short sample-to-answer analysis process in 2 min. Due to its easy operation, low-cost, and there is no pretreatment required, the capillary-based testing kit provides a new strategy for on-site and rapid detection of antibiotics in various environmental water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tetracycline
Ratiometric fluorescence
Carbonized polymer dots
Capillary testing kit
On-site detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of TC in environmental water samples
Capillary platform for visual quantitative detection of TC

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Key R&D Program of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Key Research and Development Program of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by National Key R&D Program of China ( 2018YFC1901000 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image, (B) HRTEM image, (C) XRD pattern, (D) AFM image, (E) dynamic light scattering (DLS) image of CPDs. (F) FT-IR spectra of CPDs and Eu/CPDs. (G) Full-scan XPS spectrum of CPDs. (H) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CPDs. (I) Fluorescence excitation (black line) and emission spectra of CPDs. Inset showed images of CPDs solution under day-light (left) and 365 nm UV lamp (right).
Fluorescence emission spectra of CPDs (black curve), Eu/CPDs (blue curve), CPDs + TC (pink curve) and Eu/CPDs + TC (red curve). Reaction condition: 100 μM TC, 10 μL Eu/CPDs, 1 mM Tris-HCl, pH 6.5.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of Eu3+ solution without (blue curve) and with TC (black curve), and Eu/CPDs solution in the presence of 100 μΜ TC (red curve). (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of Eu/CPDs (green curve), Eu/CPDs + TC (red curve), Eu-TC (blue curve), Eu3+ (orange curve) and TC (pink curve). (C) Fluorescence lifetime of Eu-TC (black curve) and Eu/CPDs at 618 nm in the presence of 50 μΜ TC (red curve). (D) Fluorescence lifetime of CPDs (red curve), Eu/CPDs (blue curve) and Eu/CPDs at 506 nm in the presence of 50 μΜ TC (pink curve).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of Eu/CPDs-based sensing system in the presence of different concentration of TC. (B) Linear relationship of fluorescence intensity ratio (F 620/F 506) versus TC concentration. (C) The stability experiment of Eu/CPDs response towards TC (100 μM). (D) Fluorescence intensity ratio (F 620/F 506) for Eu/CPDs in the presence of various inorganic ions, biomolecules and antibiotics. The concentration of TC and other interferences were 50 μM and 500 μM, respectively. Reaction conditions: 10 μL Eu/CPDs, 1 mM Tris-HCl, pH 6.5.

(A) Schematic assembly of the capillary testing kit. (B) The photo of capillary device under daylight. (C) Photo of capillary device under 365 nm UV flashlight. (D) Photographs of an experimenter sampling in lake water using the capillary-based sensor. (E) Photos of capillaries loading water samples spiked with different concentrations of TC under 365 nm UV flashlight. (F) Fitting curve between fluorescence chromaticity value (R/G) and TC concentration.
Schematic illustration of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on Eu/CPDs.
Comparison of Eu-based fluorescent tetracycline sensors reported in previous studies.

head.ce:title: A facile rapid-response and on-site sensor for tetracycline detection in environmental water based on europium-doped carbonized polymer dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Ecological and Environmental Sciences, Shanghai Engineering Research Center of Biotransformation of Organic Solid Waste, Shanghai Key Lab for Urban Ecological Process and Eco-Restoration, East China Normal University, 500 Dongchuan Road, Shanghai 200241, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Ecological and Environmental Sciences, Shanghai Engineering Research Center of Biotransformation of Organic Solid Waste, Shanghai Key Lab for Urban Ecological Process and Eco-Restoration, East China Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Ecological and Environmental Sciences, Shanghai Engineering Research Center of Biotransformation of Organic Solid Waste, Shanghai Key Lab for Urban Ecological Process and Eco-Restoration, East China Normal University, 500 Dongchuan Road, Shanghai 200241, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Eco-Chongming (IEC), 3663 North Zhongshan Road, Shanghai 20062, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Eco-Chongming (IEC), 3663 North Zhongshan Road, Shanghai 20062, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Ecological and Environmental Sciences, Shanghai Engineering Research Center of Biotransformation of Organic Solid Waste, Shanghai Key Lab for Urban Ecological Process and Eco-Restoration, East China Normal University, 500 Dongchuan Road, Shanghai 200241, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Ecological and Environmental Sciences, Shanghai Engineering Research Center of Biotransformation of Organic Solid Waste, Shanghai Key Lab for Urban Ecological Process and Eco-Restoration, East China Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the increasing concerns about public safety and environmental protection, it is of great significance to establish a rapid and sensitive method to detect antibiotic residues in environmental samples. Herein, fluorescent carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) were synthesized and used for quantitative assay of tetracycline (TC) by chelating with europium ions (Eu3+). The green fluorescence of CPDs could be gradually quenched by TC via inner filter effect and static quenching mechanism, while the red emission of Eu3+ was remarkably enhanced through energy transfer and antenna effect. This ratiometric fluorescence Eu/CPDs-based sensor displayed a wide detection range of 0.05–56, 56–100 μM with a detection limit of 16.4 nM, and was triumphantly applied for TC analysis in water samples with high reliability and accuracy. Moreover, the polymeric structure in CPDs endowed the sensor with good long-term sensing stability up to 600 days. By integrating Eu/CPDs with portable capillary testing kit, a point-of-use device was constructed for facile determination of TC in river water samples with a short sample-to-answer analysis process in 2 min. Due to its easy operation, low-cost, and there is no pretreatment required, the capillary-based testing kit provides a new strategy for on-site and rapid detection of antibiotics in various environmental water samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TC) is a class of broad-spectrum antibiotic and has been extensively used in animal husbandry and treatment of infectious diseases in humans due to its low-cost and favorable antibacterial activity [1]. The annual consumption of TC was estimated to be about 5000 metric tons [2]. However, long-term intake and abuse of TC have led to its accumulation in ground and surface water, soils, as well as dairy food products [3]. The antibiotic residue would bring adverse effects on ecosystem, promote bacterial resistance, and seriously threaten public safety [4]. In order to trace the residue timely and reduce its environmental impact, it is significant to develop a rapid, facile and sensitive assay for on-site detection of TC.

Over the last few decades, many analytical techniques have been established for TC detection, including high-performance liquid chromatography [5], immunoassay [6], electrochemical analysis [7] and capillary electrophoresis [8]. Although these methods provide accurate and quantitative results, they always require complicated pretreatment, expensive instrumentation, and skilled operators, which are time-consuming and unsuitable for on-site assays in complex metrics. Recently, fluorometry method has received significant attention and is considered as an effective approach owing to its prompt response and high sensitivity [9,10]. The monochromatic fluorescence sensor can be constructed attributing to the interactions between TC and diverse nanomaterials, such as metal-organic frameworks [11], metal nanoclusters [12] and nanoparticles [13]. However, these detection methods usually depend on single signal variation, which might be readily influenced by the interference of background, probe concentration and environmental factors [10]. Fortunately, ratiometric fluorescence (FL) sensing employed the FL ratio signal, which can effectively eliminate the interferences and enhance the sensitivity and accuracy of the system [14]. In this regard, europium ion (Eu3+)-based ratiometric sensors have been widely used in TC detection [15–19]. Ascribed to the β-diketonate configuration of TC, it could chelate with Eu3+ and transfer its energy to Eu3+ through antenna effect (AE), resulting in the fluorescence enhancement of Eu3+ [16]. For example, by utilizing a dual-emission fluorescence probe containing boron-doped carbon quantum dot and Eu3+ modified cadmium telluride, TC and Fe3+ can be simultaneously detected in surface water samples [15]. Han et al. reported a ratiometric sensor for the determination of TC by chelating Eu3+ onto cadmium telluride quantum dots and realized visual detection of 200 nM TC in milk samples [17]. The selective and label-free method can be designed for quantitative analysis of TC based on lanthanide coordination polymer nanoparticles [18] or g-C3N4 nanosheets with the aid of Eu3+ and CitNa [19]. Nevertheless, some of the Eu3+-based ratiometric sensors suffered from poor stability or instrumentation relied, which greatly hindered the widespread adoption of these sensors in practical environmental analysis. Hence, a facile analytical strategy for simple, sensitive and efficient sensing of TC is highly desired.

Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) with polymer-chain/carbon-core hybrid structure, an emerging class of carbon dots (CDs), with the features like good solubility, ideal stability and environmentally friendly, have attracted promising interests in fluorescent sensing and bioimaging fields [20–22]. For instance, a type of biomass-derived CPDs with optical stability and large Stokes shift (139 nm) were utilized for selective detection of levofloxacin and Cu2+ in lake and tap water samples via a fluorescence “turn on-off” manner [20]. Water-soluble poly(ether amine)-based fluorescent polymer carbon dots were synthesized and used for Fe3+ sensing in industrial effluents with a detection limit of 162 nM [22]. However, these studies mainly focused on the fluorescence variation of pure CPDs, the preparation of new CPDs with ratiometric performance should be investigated with further step. On account of the unique sensing performances of CPDs, doping moieties on their surface would likely exhibit simultaneous dual-signal responses and provide a sustainable and valid analytical method in the detection of targets. In addition, considering the requirements of sensitive and on-site detection of antibiotics in environmental emergencies and remote locations, it is highly in need to fabricate a facile and fast sensor by extending the application territory of CPDs in combinations with convenient devices. In previous research, various portable sensing platforms have been constructed with miniature instrumentations [23]. Even so, their practical applications are still limited because of complicated sample pretreatment process and the requirement of additional pumps. Therefore, it will be an ideal candidate to rationally couple CPDs with Eu3+ into a ratiometric fluorescence sensor and load it in a sealed testing kit. By virtue of this merit, the newly constructed system would not only offer a distinguished color gradients towards TC, but also provide a more flexible design by integrating all the steps of purification, collection, mixing and reaction in one device.

Herein, a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensing system was proposed for TC detection based on highly stable and water-soluble Eu/CPDs. As depicted in Scheme 1, CPDs were synthesized through a facile two-step hydrothermal method and subsequently chelated with Eu3+ as a dual-response probe. With the addition of TC, the green emission of CPDs can be gradually quenched, whereas the characteristic red emission of Eu3+ was remarkably enhanced. These target-triggered effects could cause a reverse variation at two FL wavelengths and an obvious fluorescence color change from green to red with increasing concentrations of TC. The developed ratiometric fluorescence sensor displayed quick response, high sensitivity, and good stability for TC detection. Moreover, by encapsulating Eu/CPDs into a portable capillary, a point-of-use testing kit was developed for on-site visual quantitative monitoring of TC and could achieve a facile sample-in-answer-out process in one device. The proposed detection strategy may overcome the shortcomings of high-cost, instrumentation-relied and pretreatment-required, as well as provide a potent approach to realize rapid analysis of antibiotics in environmental water samples.
Chemicals and regents, instrumentations, and capillary testing kit detection procedure are described in the Supporting Information.

Initially, CPDs were prepared with p-phenylenediamine, ethylene imine polymer (PEI) and γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA) as precursors via a two-step hydrothermal method [24]. 0.15 g of p-phenylenediamine and 0.15 g of PEI were completely dissolved in 10 mL water and stirred for 15 min at room temperature (25 ℃). The mixture was transferred to the Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 120 ℃ for 5 h. After the reaction was completed, the solution was naturally cooled to room temperature. Then, 0.15 g of GABA was added to the solution and stirred for another 15 min. The mixed solution was reheated at 100 ℃ for 3 h. Finally, the obtained product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm to remove large particles and the supernatant was purified using a dialysis bag (molecular weight cutoff: 500 Da) for 24 h. The solution inside the bag was collected and stored at 4 ℃ for further use.

Eu/CPDs was prepared via the chelation reaction between Eu3+ and CPDs. At first, 3 mL Eu(NO3)3·6 H2O (0.1 M) was added into the mixture of 10 mL CPDs and 12 mL water. Thereafter, the solution was oscillated at 600 rpm for 3 h at room temperature. Then, the Eu/CPDs solution was dialyzed (500 Da) for 5 h to remove excess europium salts. The resultant pale-brown product was obtained and stored at 4 ℃ before use.

Briefly, 10 μL Eu/CPDs was mixed with different concentrations of TC. Thereafter, the mixture was diluted with Tris-HCl solution (1 mM, pH 6.5) to 1.0 mL and incubated at room temperature for 1 min. The fluorescence spectra were measured from 400 nm to 700 nm with the excitation wavelength of 365 nm.
Several potential interferences were performed according to above approach, including inorganic ions, biomolecules and antibiotics. Their concentration were 10 times of TC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of TC in environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The environmental water sample was obtained from Yangtze River (Shanghai, China) and was filtered through 0.22 µm membrane to remove large particles. The supernatant was obtained and analyzed according to the procedure in Section 2.3.

The synthesis conditions including reaction temperature and time, the concentration of p-phenylenediamine and PEI were optimized in the first step. As shown in Fig. S2, 120 ℃, 5 h and 15 mg mL−1 of each precursor was chosen as the most suitable condition. The secondary reaction was proceeded under a relatively mild condition to allow the introduction of more carboxyl groups for Eu coordination. According to the TEM image in Fig. 1A, the obtained CPDs had nearly spherical shape and good dispersity with an average diameter of 9.87 nm. The corresponding HRTEM image (Fig. 1B) hint a high crystallinity of CPDs and the ordered lattice fringes spacing distance of 0.21 nm was attributed to the (100) plane of the graphitic carbon [25]. Furthermore, in XRD patterns (Fig. 1C), two diffraction peaks were observed including an intense peak at 19.92˚ (d002 = 0.34 nm) and a broad peak at 43.36˚ (d100 = 0.21 nm), suggesting that CPDs consisted of an amorphous carbon phase with fractional graphitization [26]. The AFM result in Fig. 1D validated the morphology of CPDs with an average height of ∼15 nm, which is larger than the diameter obtained by TEM. The deviation between the height in AFM and diameter in TEM proved the existence of polymer chains shell on external surface of CPDs. The size distribution was characterized by DLS (Fig. 1E) and their average diameter in water was 110.6 nm with a polydispersity index (PDI) of 0.34, which further confirmed the carbon core in the center and polymer-shell structure in CPDs [27]. The FT-IR spectrum was recorded to analyze the functional groups of the material. As displayed in Fig. 1F (red line), a broad band at 3420 cm−1 corresponding to O-H and N-H could be observed. The peak at 2896 cm−1 and 1594 cm−1 were ascribed to the vibrations of C-H and CN. The characteristic vibration peaks of CO, CC and C-O were observed at 1799 cm−1, 1632 cm−1 and 1163 cm−1, respectively. The peaks at 1430 cm−1 and 1108 cm−1 were assigned to the vibrations of C-N and C-N secondary amine group stretching. All the above results demonstrated there are abundant -NH2 and -COOH groups on the surface of CPDs, which is beneficial for chelating with Eu3+ and dispersion in water [28]. In the spectrum of Eu/CPDs (Fig. 1F, blue curve), the characteristic O-H and CO bonds were red-shifted due to the coordination of Eu3+ with the oxygen atoms in hydroxyl and carbonyl groups. Attributing to the adsorbed water molecules coordinated with Eu3+, the O-H vibration was broadened in the Eu/CPDs compared to the spectrum of pure CPDs. Moreover, a new peak belonged to Eu-O bond appeared at 439 cm−1 [29], together with an additional vibrational peak at 1384 cm−1 assigned to the free nitrate species in the material [30]. The elemental composition of CPDs was further obtained by XPS analysis. As shown in Fig. 1G, C, N and O could be found with atomic percentages of 84.51%, 9.36%, and 6.13%, respectively. From the high-resolution peak depicted in Fig. S3A, the C 1 s peak could be deconvoluted into six peaks at 284.6, 285.0, 285.7, 286.9, 287.8 and 288.8 eV, which can be assigned to CC/C-C, C-N, C-O, CN, CO and O-CO, respectively. The high-resolution peak of N 1 s in Fig. S3B can be divided into three peaks at 398.9, 399.6 and 400.6 eV, corresponding to CN-C, C-N-C and N-H, respectively. The O 1 s peak (Fig. S3C) can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.5 and 533.4 eV, attributing to the CO and C-OH/C-O-C bonds. In the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Eu/CPDs (Fig. S4), the appearance of Eu 3d peak in Fig. S4A indicated the existence of Eu 3d5/2 (1134.6 eV) and Eu 3d3/2 (1164.8 eV). Meanwhile, the peak of CO shifted to a higher binding energy of 532.0 eV (Fig. S4D) due to the oxygen coordination with Eu3+ [31]. The results in FT-IR and XPS confirmed the CPDs have been synthesized and the successful functional modification of Eu-complex on their surface.

The optical properties of CPDs were investigated by UV–vis and FL spectrum. As depicted in Fig. 1H, the weak adsorption band centered at 260 nm was ascribed to the π-π * transitions of CC, while the absorption peak at 360 nm corresponded to n-π * transitions of CO/N. In Fig. 1I, CPDs displayed excitation-independent emission when different excitation wavelengths performed from 320 nm to 400 nm. A maximum FL emission at 506 nm was observed at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The solution of CPDs was in pale-brown color under daylight (inset photo of Fig. 1I, left) and emitted bright green fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp (inset photo of Fig. 1I, right). Moreover, as shown in Fig. S5, the relative quantum yield of CPDs was measured and calculated to be 5.9%. The stability is another important parameter for fluorescence sensors, the as-prepared CPDs could maintain the fluorescence intensity in a broad pH range from 4 to 11 (Fig. S6A) and when exposed to air and/or sunlight for 5 days (Fig. S6B), revealing they have good stability compared with traditional quantum dots and can be applied for on-site detection in practical environmental conditions.

The sensing strategy is described in Scheme 1 and is validated by fluorescence measurements. As shown in Fig. 2, compared with Eu/CPDs (blue curve), the emission at 506 nm decreased remarkably, whereas the fluorescence of Eu3+ at 620 nm displayed a significant increase after introducing TC into the system (red curve). Therefore, a ratiometric fluorescent sensor can be constructed for visual quantitative of TC. Likewise, the addition of TC to CPDs was also implemented and displayed a relative fluorescence quenching at 506 nm (black curve, pink curve).

The sensing mechanism was carefully explored. Firstly, the reason behind FL quenching of CPDs by TC was discussed. As shown in Fig. S7A, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of TC overlapped well with the excitation spectrum of CPDs. The fluorescence quenching efficiency changed with different excitation wavelengths and displayed the maximum value under the absorption center of TC (Fig. S7B, Table S1), thus the possibility of inner filter effect cannot be excluded [22]. In the meanwhile, the absorption spectrum of CPDs at 360 nm displayed an obvious red shift with the increasing concentration of TC (Fig. S7C and Fig. S7D), indicating the formation of new ground-state complexes in the system [32,33]. As shown in the FT-IR result, vibration peaks of C-N (1036 cm−1), CH3 (1357 cm−1), aromatic C-H (1457 cm−1), CC (1616 cm−1), CO (1675 cm−1) and O-H/N-H (3300–3400 cm−1) appeared in the spectrum of TC (Fig. S7E, black curve). Compared with CPDs (Fig. S7E, blue curve), a red-shift and broader peak of O-H/N-H was observed at 3212.1 cm−1, demonstrating the existence of hydrogen-bonding interactions between the amino groups of CPDs and the hydroxyl groups of TC (Fig. S7E, red curve) [11]. Meanwhile, the shift of CO, C-N and CC vibrations and their decreased intensity after interacting with TC indicated that π-π electron-donor-acceptor interaction was involved between TC and CPDs [34]. When CPDs-TC complex absorbs light, the complex would immediately return to the ground-state without emission of a photon to quench the green fluorescence [32]. In addition, the fluorescence lifetime of CPDs at 506 nm revealed bi-exponential behavior and remained almost unchanged after adding various TC concentrations (Fig. S7F, Table S2). Based on above results, the existence of static quenching effect was confirmed in the system. The FL quenching efficiency can be described by the Stern-Volmer plot followed Eq. (1),
Eq. (1)
F
0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
C
q
= 1 +
K
q
τ
0
C
q
Where F 0 and F represent the FL intensity of CPDs in the absence and presence of TC, Ksv is the quenching constant, Cq is TC concentration, τ 0 is the FL lifetime of CPDs, Kq is the quenching rate constant. Here, the FL intensity of CPDs was gradually quenched with the increasing concentration of TC (Fig. S8A) and the Ksv value was obtained to be 0.00865 μM−1 from the linear relationship depicted in Fig. S8B. Kq was calculated to be 1.47 × 1012 M−1 s−1 on the basis of Ksv and τ 0 (5.87 ns) and it was much larger than the value in dynamic quenching effect (Kq < 2 × 1010 M−1 s−1), indicating the existence of some binding interactions [35]. All the above results verified the fluorescence quenching of CPDs by TC was related to static quenching mechanism along with an inner filter effect.

Subsequently, the FL enhancing principle of Eu3+ was discussed. As demonstrated in Fig. 3A, a sharp emission peak at 620 nm appeared in the spectrum of Eu/CPDs with the addition of TC (red curve), and the intensity was 12 times and 225 times as that of Eu-TC (black curve) and Eu3+ (blue curve), respectively. The sharp peak was ascribed to the hypersensitive transition of Eu3+ from 5D0 to 7F2 and its enhancement could be explained from three parts. Firstly, after chelating with Eu3+, the β-diketonate configuration in TC enabled it to absorb efficient energy and transfer it to Eu3+, sensitizing its luminescence through antenna effect [36]. The formation of Eu-TC complex can be validated by the red-shifted absorption peak in UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 3B, pink and blue curve). Secondly, TC could replace the coordination water molecules on europium, thus inhibiting the non-radiation quenching from high frequency OH oscillators [37]. In addition, compared with TC, Eu-TC and Eu/CPDs (Fig. 3B, pink, blue and green curve), the absorption peak of Eu/CPDs with TC was significantly red-shifted (red curve), revealing the formation of Eu/CPDs-TC ternary complex. As shown in Fig. 3C and Table S3, the fluorescent lifetime of Eu-TC was obviously prolonged from 9.36 μs to 25.44 μs in the presence of CPDs, which can be ascribed to the CPDs can further expel the water molecules coordinated to Eu3+ and avoid nonradiative energy loss in the complexes [18,38]. Moreover, since the FL emission spectrum of CPDs had little overlap with the absorption spectrum of Eu-TC (Fig. S9), the possible contribution of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) could be very small [39]. The shortened fluorescence lifetime of CPDs in the presence of Eu-TC (Fig. 3D and Table S4) can be caused by coordination interaction between CPDs and Eu-TC, thus promoted energy transfer from CPDs to Eu3+ and enhanced the FL intensity in red emission [17,40]. In the Eu/CPDs-TC ternary complex, Eu3+ was one recognition unit and energy receptor, while CPDs served as another sensing unit and energy donor for the enhancement of Eu-TC fluorescence.

To obtain the best analytical performance, several relevant variables were investigated, including Eu3+ concentration, pH value and the reaction time. As shown in Fig. S10, the best response of Eu/CPDs toward TC was observed with 12 μM Eu3+. Besides, the effect of pH was explored and the maximum ratiometric signal occurred at pH 5.0–6.5 (Fig. S11A). The fluorescence quenching efficiency of Eu/CPDs at 506 nm decreased in alkaline medium due to the transformation of TC (Fig. S11B). The optimal enhancement at 620 nm was observed at pH 6.5 because the protonation of TC under strong acidic conditions was unfavorable for energy transfer to Eu3+, whereas under alkaline conditions, the precipitation of europium hydroxide would decrease the sensitivity of the assay [37,41]. Finally, the fluorescence intensity of the sensor was investigated over time after adding TC to the Eu/CPDs solution. As shown in Fig. S11C, the fluorescence ratio signal increased rapidly in 1 min and tended to be stable within 5 min, demonstrating the sensing system has great potential for rapid and on-site detection of TC. Based on above results, a pH value of 6.5 and a reaction time of 1 min were selected as the optimal conditions in subsequent tests.

Under the optimal noted conditions, the response performance of Eu/CPDs-based sensor was evaluated. As displayed in Fig. 4A, with the increasing concentrations of TC from 0 to 100 μM, the fluorescence at 506 nm progressively decreased whereas the peak at 620 nm gradually increased, accompanied by the luminescence color change from green to red (inset photo of Fig. 4A). A good linear relationship can be obtained between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F 620/F 506) and TC concentrations in the range of 0.05–56 μM and 56–100 μM, with a correlation coefficient (R 2) both of 0.998 (Fig. 4B). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 16.4 nM according to 3σ rule, which is much lower than the maximum residue limit level of TC (225 nM) set by the European Union [19]. Compared with previous reported europium-based TC sensors ( Table 1), the developed sensor has a wide linear range and a convenient assay process. Notably, attributing to sufficient binding sites in CPDs for TC combination together with the high affinity between TC and Eu3+, the short response time within 1 min allowed the sensor to be applied in rapid TC analysis. More importantly, the Eu/CPDs-based sensor could maintain 96.5% of its stability after 600 days (Fig. 4C). Such good stability performance may attribute to the protection provided by core-shell CPDs with polymeric chains structure as well as their admirable dispersibility with abundant functional groups, which would prevent aggregations of the probe after long-term of storage [46]. These experimental results demonstrated the proposed Eu/CPDs-based sensor offered a robust tool for sensitive and on-site detection of TC, thus could be further used in real environmental applications.

Furthermore, the selectivity of the sensor was elucidated by screening its response toward various ions, competitive molecules, and abundant antibiotics at even higher concentrations. As displayed in Fig. 4D, all the possible interfering substances have no obvious effects on the fluorescence intensity of the system except the TC class antibiotics, indicating the developed Eu/CPDs-based sensor has good selectivity. As for doxycycline, oxytetracycline and chlortetracycline, they can also be detected by the proposed sensing method because they belonged to the same tetracycline family with similar chemical structures as TC (Fig. S12).

To investigate the applicability and reliability of the sensor, environmental water sample was collected and used after pretreatment. The related results were summarized in Table S5. Since no TC was detected in the sample, the standard addition recovery experiment was performed with spiked levels of 5.00, 10.00, and 20.00 μM to further evaluate the reliability of our method. Good recoveries from 95.7% to 109.8% with relative standard deviation (RSD) below 8.4% were obtained, demonstrating the developed sensing strategy possessed satisfactory accuracy and reliability in environmental water analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Capillary platform for visual quantitative detection of TC
ce:section.ce:para: Combining the well-designed performance of Eu/CPDs-based sensor, a portable capillary testing kit was fabricated to simplify the pretreatment and detection procedures, which could fulfill the urgent requirements of on-the-spot analysis. Fig. 5A, B and C illustrated the schematic of the sensor assembly, the photo of capillary device under natural daylight and 365 nm UV-flashlight, respectively. Glass capillary was chosen due to its features of favorable transparency and stable physicochemical properties. The environmental water sample was obtained and analyzed without any pretreatment (Fig. 5D). A smartphone was used as an image reader to capture the capillary photos loading river water samples spiked with different TC concentrations (Fig. 5E). Impressively, the fluorescent color changes of the capillaries induced by TC could be visually discriminated, and quantitative analysis can be achieved without any bulky instrumentations. As shown in Fig. 5F, the signal variation of RGB values (red (R)/green (G)) linearly depended on TC concentrations in the range of 5–100 μM (R 2 = 0.997) with a low LOD of 2.8 μM according to the 3σ rule. The above results confirmed the capillary-based testing kit exhibited properties of all-in-one compact, facile detection steps and short sample-to-answer process within 2 min, indicating the potential practicality for on-site determination of TC in environmental samples. More importantly, the constructed instrument-free sensing strategy integrated all the detection elements together, thus significantly simplified the operation process and gave promising insights for in-field analysis. In comparison with other portable sensing platforms, the as-developed novel capillary testing kit is more convenient for storage and can achieve rapid quantitative detection at a lower cost. All the results demonstrated the capillary-based testing kit is particularly suitable for point-of-use analysis of environmental contaminates in daily or emergent regulations and less industrialized areas.

In summary, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor was rationally designed and constructed for TC detection by chelating Eu3+ on CPDs. The highly stable and water-soluble CPDs serve as both a recognition unit and an energy donor to enhance the emission of Eu3+. Thanks to the effective quenching of TC toward CPDs and dramatic fluorescence enhancement of CPDs toward Eu-TC, the proposed sensor realized selective and sensitive determination of TC with a short response time of 1 min and a wide linear range of 0.05–56, 56–100 μM. The sensing system was applied in the analysis of environmental water samples with good reliability. Moreover, the developed pump-free capillary testing kit integrated Eu/CPDs-based sensing elements in one device and realized a point-of-use analytical process within 2 min in a simple sample-to-answer manner. Particularly, the facile fluorescence sensor presented advances such as low-cost, easy-to-operate and rapid response. The designed analytical strategy provided the possibility for practical applications in the on-site visual quantitative monitoring of environmental contaminants by non-specialist consumers.

Qi Nie: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Jingjing Deng: Methodology, Data curation. Tianshu Zhou: Data curation, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National Key R&D Program of China (2018YFC1901000).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2023.109900.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
